entities.name: vyavastha
entities.cite: nobody codifies an anarthika, purposeless, system

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: so it must have a purpose

entities.name: smṛti
entities.cite: the sistas plainly did codify one

entities.name: dharma
entities.cite: that purpose is dharma rather than communication

entities.name: vyavahāra
entities.cite: that purpose is dharma rather than communication

axioms.name: āśrayate
axioms.cite: nobody codifies an anarthika, purposeless, system

axioms.name: sampad
axioms.cite: so it must have a purpose
that purpose is dharma rather than communication

denials.cite: that purpose is dharma rather than communication

claims.name: sampad
claims.cite: No one would undertake this arrangement if it served no purpose

rejected_readings.label: anarthika_tradition
rejected_readings.rendering: The tradition concerning correctness could be purposeless if properly established.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the causal principle: the verse establishes that IF something is codified, it MUST have purpose. To say smṛti could be anarthika contradicts the logical framework the verse uses—the fact of its composition by the cultivated entails it must serve an artha.

rejected_readings.label: communication_purpose
rejected_readings.rendering: The tradition of correctness serves the purpose of enabling communication.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly rules this out: 'that purpose is dharma rather than communication'. The artha of smṛti is dharma (righteous behavior/linguistic purity in conduct), not mere vyavahāra (communicative function). This reading confuses the vehicle (language) with the destination (dharma).

notes: The verse establishes a deductive principle (codified systems must serve purpose) and applies it to resolve that smṛti is not purposeless. The identification artha = dharma is cited from the commentary's reference to 1.27, grounding the philosophical move from logical argument to soteriological claim. The sorts treat vyavastha as manifestation (it is the structured expression), smṛti as linguisticItem (it is textual tradition), and both artha and dharma as absolute (they are the ultimate goals—the purpose itself and righteous conduct).
